content.text: Cross-attention is a variant of the scaled dot-product attention operation in which the query (Q) and the key-value pair (K, V) come from different sequences. In self-attention, Q, K, and V are all projections of the same input sequence. In cross-attention, Q is derived from a "target" sequence — the one being generated or processed — while K and V are derived from a separate "source" sequence that provides context.

This asymmetry is exactly what multimodal fusion needs: one modality (text) generates queries that retrieve relevant information from another modality (images), without the two needing to share a common token space. The mechanism is the same attention kernel used everywhere in transformers — only the origin of Q vs K/V differs.

attrs.alt: Cross-attention formula and three multimodal use cases: Flamingo (text attends to Perceiver latents), Stable Diffusion UNet (spatial features attend to CLIP text), BLIP-2 Q-Former (learnable queries attend to ViT patches).

content.text: For a target sequence of length T and source sequence of length S, each with embedding dimension d:
is the per-head dimension for h attention heads. The attention weight matrix has shape

— each target position attends over all S source positions. The output has shape T × d, same as the target sequence — the cross-attention operation enriches each target token with information drawn from the source.
scaling prevents the dot products from growing large in magnitude as d increases, which would push the softmax into saturation (near-zero gradients). The cost of the attention matrix computation is
— importantly, this scales with the product of the two sequence lengths rather than their sum squared as in full joint self-attention over a concatenated sequence.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

class MultiHeadCrossAttention(nn.Module):
    """
    Multi-head cross-attention: Q from target modality, K/V from source modality.
    Used in Flamingo, DALL-E 2 decoder, Stable Diffusion UNet, BLIP-2 Q-Former.
    """
    def __init__(self, d_model: int = 1024, n_heads: int = 8,
                 src_dim: int = None, dropout: float = 0.0):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % n_heads == 0
        self.n_heads = n_heads
        self.d_head  = d_model // n_heads
        src_dim = src_dim or d_model

        # Q comes from the target sequence
        self.W_q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        # K, V come from the source sequence (may have different dim)
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(src_dim, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(src_dim, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_o = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(
        self,
        tgt: torch.Tensor,   # (B, T, d_model)  — target (e.g. text hidden states)
        src: torch.Tensor,   # (B, S, src_dim)   — source (e.g. image features)
        src_mask: torch.Tensor = None,  # (B, 1, 1, S) boolean mask for padding
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        B, T, _ = tgt.shape
        S = src.shape[1]

        # Project each sequence to multi-head Q / K / V
        q = self.W_q(tgt).view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, h, T, d_h)
        k = self.W_k(src).view(B, S, self.n_heads, self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, h, S, d_h)
        v = self.W_v(src).view(B, S, self.n_heads, self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, h, S, d_h)

        # Scaled dot-product attention
        scale  = math.sqrt(self.d_head)
        scores = torch.matmul(q, k.transpose(-2, -1)) / scale   # (B, h, T, S)

        if src_mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(src_mask, float("-inf"))

        weights = self.dropout(F.softmax(scores, dim=-1))        # (B, h, T, S)
        out = torch.matmul(weights, v)                           # (B, h, T, d_h)

        # Merge heads
        out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, -1)   # (B, T, d_model)
        return self.W_o(out)                                      # (B, T, d_model)


# Usage: text tokens attend to image patch features
B, T, S = 2, 32, 196   # 32 text tokens, 196 image patches
text_hidden   = torch.randn(B, T, 1024)
image_patches = torch.randn(B, S, 768)  # different source dim

cross_attn = MultiHeadCrossAttention(d_model=1024, n_heads=8, src_dim=768)
output = cross_attn(text_hidden, image_patches)
print(output.shape)   # (2, 32, 1024) — text tokens enriched with visual context
Flamingo (Alayrac et al., 2022) introduced a design for inserting cross-attention into a frozen LLM without catastrophically disrupting its language capabilities. The key insight is
: each cross-attention layer's output is multiplied by

where α is a learnable scalar parameter initialised to zero. At the start of training, the gate is zero — the LLM behaves identically to its pre-trained state. As training progresses, α learns the right amplitude for visual conditioning.

Cross-attention layers are interleaved with the frozen LLM's own self-attention blocks, one per transformer block. The LLM's weights are frozen; only the new cross-attention layers and the Perceiver Resampler (which compresses image patch tokens to 64 fixed latents) are trained. This makes Flamingo an extreme case of parameter-efficient multimodal training.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class GatedCrossAttentionBlock(nn.Module):
    """
    Flamingo-style gated cross-attention block, inserted between frozen LLM blocks.
    Reference: Alayrac et al. "Flamingo: a Visual Language Model for Few-Shot Learning" (2022).
    """
    def __init__(self, d_model: int = 4096, n_heads: int = 32, visual_dim: int = 1024):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm_q = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)    # normalise text query
        self.cross_attn = MultiHeadCrossAttention(
            d_model=d_model, n_heads=n_heads, src_dim=visual_dim
        )
        # Gate: tanh(alpha) * attn_output — alpha=0 → no visual signal at init
        self.alpha_attn = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1))

        # Gated FFN after cross-attention
        self.norm_ff = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.ff = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_model * 4),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(d_model * 4, d_model),
        )
        self.alpha_ff = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1))

    def forward(
        self,
        text_hidden: torch.Tensor,       # (B, T, d_model) — text tokens from LLM
        visual_features: torch.Tensor,  # (B, K, visual_dim) — Perceiver latents
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        # Cross-attention: text queries attend to visual keys/values
        q_normed = self.norm_q(text_hidden)
        attn_out = self.cross_attn(q_normed, visual_features)
        # Gate output — starts at 0, LLM output is unaffected at beginning of training
        text_hidden = text_hidden + torch.tanh(self.alpha_attn) * attn_out

        # Gated FFN
        ff_out = self.ff(self.norm_ff(text_hidden))
        text_hidden = text_hidden + torch.tanh(self.alpha_ff) * ff_out

        return text_hidden


class FlamingoBridge(nn.Module):
    """
    Wraps a frozen LLM and inserts gated cross-attention blocks between its layers.
    In Flamingo-80B this adds 10 cross-attn blocks to a 70-layer frozen Chinchilla LLM.
    """
    def __init__(self, n_llm_layers: int = 32, d_model: int = 4096, visual_dim: int = 1024):
        super().__init__()
        self.cross_attn_blocks = nn.ModuleList([
            GatedCrossAttentionBlock(d_model=d_model, visual_dim=visual_dim)
            for _ in range(n_llm_layers // 4)  # every 4 LLM layers gets a cross-attn
        ])

    def apply_to_layer(
        self,
        layer_idx: int,
        hidden: torch.Tensor,
        visual: torch.Tensor,
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Called after every 4th LLM layer if a cross-attn block exists for it."""
        block_idx = layer_idx // 4
        if block_idx < len(self.cross_attn_blocks):
            return self.cross_attn_blocks[block_idx](hidden, visual)
        return hidden

Stable Diffusion (Rombach et al., 2022) uses cross-attention to condition image generation on a text prompt at every UNet block. Here the roles are reversed: the spatial feature maps of the noisy image provide the queries, and the CLIP text encoder's 77-token output provides the keys and values. At each denoising step, every spatial location in the image can attend to all 77 text tokens to decide which pixels to adjust.

Because the text is fixed across all denoising steps, its K and V projections can be computed once and cached for the entire generation trajectory — only the spatial Q projections change at each step.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

class SpatialTransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    """
    Cross-attention block from Stable Diffusion's UNet.
    Conditions 2D spatial feature maps on a text embedding sequence.
    Reference: Rombach et al. "High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models" (2022).
    """
    def __init__(
        self,
        spatial_dim: int = 512,   # feature map channel dim
        context_dim: int = 768,   # CLIP text encoder output dim (ViT-L/14: 768)
        n_heads: int = 8,
    ):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(spatial_dim)
        self.to_q = nn.Linear(spatial_dim, spatial_dim, bias=False)
        self.to_k = nn.Linear(context_dim, spatial_dim, bias=False)
        self.to_v = nn.Linear(context_dim, spatial_dim, bias=False)
        self.to_out = nn.Linear(spatial_dim, spatial_dim)
        self.n_heads = n_heads
        self.d_head  = spatial_dim // n_heads

    def forward(
        self,
        x: torch.Tensor,           # (B, H*W, spatial_dim) — flattened feature map
        context: torch.Tensor,     # (B, 77, context_dim)  — CLIP text embedding
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        B, HW, _ = x.shape
        x_norm = self.norm(x)

        # Spatial features → Q; text → K, V
        q = self.to_q(x_norm)
        k = self.to_k(context)
        v = self.to_v(context)

        # Reshape for multi-head: (B, HW or 77, h, d_h) → (B*h, T, d_h)
        def reshape(t):
            return t.view(B, -1, self.n_heads, self.d_head)                    .permute(0, 2, 1, 3)                    .reshape(B * self.n_heads, -1, self.d_head)

        q, k, v = reshape(q), reshape(k), reshape(v)
        scale   = math.sqrt(self.d_head)
        attn    = F.softmax(torch.bmm(q, k.transpose(-2, -1)) / scale, dim=-1)
        out     = torch.bmm(attn, v)                             # (B*h, HW, d_h)
        out     = out.view(B, self.n_heads, HW, self.d_head)                     .permute(0, 2, 1, 3)                     .reshape(B, HW, -1)
        return x + self.to_out(out)    # residual connection


# Typical use in UNet forward pass
def unet_block_forward(spatial_feats, text_embs, block):
    B, C, H, W = spatial_feats.shape
    flat  = spatial_feats.view(B, C, H * W).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, HW, C)
    out   = block(flat, text_embs)
    return out.transpose(1, 2).view(B, C, H, W)

# Text embeddings cached once; reused at every denoising step
text_embs = torch.randn(1, 77, 768)   # from CLIP text encoder
block     = SpatialTransformerBlock(spatial_dim=512, context_dim=768)
feats     = torch.randn(1, 512, 16, 16)   # 16×16 feature map (64×64 latent)
conditioned = unet_block_forward(feats, text_embs, block)
print(conditioned.shape)   # (1, 512, 16, 16)
BLIP-2 (Li et al., 2023) uses cross-attention as a

rather than a conditioning mechanism. The Q-Former (Querying Transformer) contains a fixed set of 32 learnable query tokens. These queries attend to the frozen ViT's 196+ patch tokens via cross-attention, extracting a compact 32-token summary of the image. This summary is then fed into the LLM as a prefix.

The architecture also includes self-attention among the 32 query tokens, enabling them to coordinate and avoid redundancy. Three training objectives operate simultaneously on the Q-Former: image-text contrastive loss (like CLIP), image-grounded text generation (autoregressive from queries), and image-text matching (binary classification). Only the Q-Former and a linear projection are trained; the ViT and LLM are frozen.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class QFormer(nn.Module):
    """
    BLIP-2 Q-Former: 32 learnable queries extract a fixed-size summary
    from frozen image patch tokens via cross-attention.
    Reference: Li et al. "BLIP-2: Bootstrapping Language-Image Pre-training" (2023).
    """
    def __init__(
        self,
        n_queries: int = 32,
        d_model: int = 768,
        visual_dim: int = 1408,   # ViT-G/14 output dim
        n_heads: int = 12,
        n_layers: int = 12,
    ):
        super().__init__()
        # 32 learnable query tokens (initialised randomly, trained)
        self.query_tokens = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(1, n_queries, d_model) * 0.02)

        # Project visual features to Q-Former dim
        self.visual_proj = nn.Linear(visual_dim, d_model, bias=False)

        # Alternating self-attention and cross-attention layers
        self.self_attns   = nn.ModuleList([
            nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, n_heads, batch_first=True)
            for _ in range(n_layers)
        ])
        self.cross_attns  = nn.ModuleList([
            nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, n_heads, batch_first=True)
            for _ in range(n_layers)
        ])
        self.ff_layers    = nn.ModuleList([
            nn.Sequential(
                nn.LayerNorm(d_model),
                nn.Linear(d_model, d_model * 4),
                nn.GELU(),
                nn.Linear(d_model * 4, d_model),
            )
            for _ in range(n_layers)
        ])
        self.norms_self   = nn.ModuleList([nn.LayerNorm(d_model) for _ in range(n_layers)])
        self.norms_cross  = nn.ModuleList([nn.LayerNorm(d_model) for _ in range(n_layers)])

    def forward(self, image_feats: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        image_feats: (B, S, visual_dim) — frozen ViT patch tokens
        Returns: (B, 32, d_model) — compressed image queries for LLM
        """
        B = image_feats.size(0)
        vis = self.visual_proj(image_feats)   # (B, S, d_model)

        # Expand learnable query tokens for batch
        queries = self.query_tokens.expand(B, -1, -1)  # (B, 32, d_model)

        for sa, ca, ff, n_sa, n_ca in zip(
            self.self_attns, self.cross_attns, self.ff_layers,
            self.norms_self, self.norms_cross
        ):
            # 1. Self-attention among queries: coordinates their information
            q_norm = n_sa(queries)
            sa_out, _ = sa(q_norm, q_norm, q_norm)
            queries = queries + sa_out

            # 2. Cross-attention: queries (Q) ← image patches (K, V)
            q_norm = n_ca(queries)
            ca_out, _ = ca(q_norm, vis, vis)
            queries = queries + ca_out

            # 3. Feed-forward
            queries = queries + ff(queries)

        return queries   # (B, 32, d_model) — fixed-size image summary


# Stage 2: project Q-Former output to LLM embedding dim
class QFormerToLLM(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, qformer_dim: int = 768, llm_dim: int = 4096):
        super().__init__()
        self.qformer    = QFormer(d_model=qformer_dim)
        self.linear_proj = nn.Linear(qformer_dim, llm_dim)

    def get_image_prefix(self, image_feats: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        q_out    = self.qformer(image_feats)           # (B, 32, 768)
        llm_proj = self.linear_proj(q_out)             # (B, 32, 4096)
        return llm_proj  # prepend to text embeddings in LLM

A key application of cross-attention in text-to-image generation is Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG, Ho & Salimans, 2022). During training, the text conditioning is randomly dropped (replaced with an empty/null embedding) with probability p ≈ 0.1, training the model to denoise both conditionally and unconditionally. At inference, both conditional and unconditional scores are computed and combined:

where c is the text embedding (source of K, V in the UNet cross-attention), ∅ is the null/unconditional embedding, and w is the guidance scale (typically 7.5). Higher w produces images more faithfully adhering to the prompt but with less diversity. The two forward passes can be batched together for efficiency.

import torch

def classifier_free_guidance_step(
    model,          # noise prediction model with cross-attention conditioning
    x_t: torch.Tensor,          # (B, C, H, W) noisy latent at step t
    text_emb: torch.Tensor,     # (B, 77, 768) — conditional text embedding
    null_emb: torch.Tensor,     # (B, 77, 768) — unconditional (empty prompt) embedding
    t: torch.Tensor,            # (B,) diffusion timestep
    guidance_scale: float = 7.5,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Compute guided noise estimate by combining conditional and unconditional predictions.
    Cross-attention K, V come from text_emb (conditional) or null_emb (unconditional).
    """
    # Batch both forward passes together for efficiency
    x_doubled   = torch.cat([x_t, x_t], dim=0)           # (2B, C, H, W)
    emb_doubled = torch.cat([null_emb, text_emb], dim=0)  # (2B, 77, 768)
    t_doubled   = torch.cat([t, t], dim=0)                # (2B,)

    # Single batched forward pass through UNet
    noise_pred = model(x_doubled, t_doubled, context=emb_doubled)   # (2B, C, H, W)
    eps_uncond, eps_cond = noise_pred.chunk(2, dim=0)

    # Classifier-free guidance: steer toward conditional, away from unconditional
    eps_guided = eps_uncond + guidance_scale * (eps_cond - eps_uncond)
    return eps_guided

# Guidance scale controls the trade-off:
# w=1.0  → no guidance (pure unconditional)
# w=7.5  → strong text adherence (typical default)
# w=15+  → over-saturated / artifacts
In early fusion (token concatenation), the shared self-attention operates over a joint sequence of length T + S, giving attention cost
. When T ≪ S (e.g., 32 query tokens vs 196 image patches), cross-attention is much cheaper than concatenating all tokens into a single self-attention stream.
T text tokens × 64 latents — K fixed

The Q-Former bottleneck (32 queries) reduces cost by 18× compared to direct concatenation of 576 patch tokens, enabling BLIP-2 to feed any LLM at fixed token cost regardless of image resolution. The trade-off is compression loss: fine-grained spatial details that don't make it into the 32-token summary are invisible to the LLM.

Standard positional encodings are applied independently to each modality's sequence before the Q/K/V projections. For image patches, 2D positional encodings (row + column indices embedded separately and summed) preserve spatial structure. For text, the standard learned or sinusoidal 1D encoding is used.

In cross-attention the positions interact only through the attention weight matrix — a query at text position i attends to source positions 0…S-1 with weights influenced by the source's positional encodings. There is no requirement that text and image positional spaces be aligned or share a common encoding scheme.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class Learned2DPosEncoding(nn.Module):
    """
    2D positional encoding for image patch sequences.
    Row and column embeddings are summed and applied before cross-attention K/V projection.
    Used in ViT variants and multimodal patch-position encoding.
    """
    def __init__(self, max_rows: int = 24, max_cols: int = 24, d_model: int = 768):
        super().__init__()
        self.row_embed = nn.Embedding(max_rows, d_model // 2)
        self.col_embed = nn.Embedding(max_cols, d_model // 2)

    def forward(self, H: int, W: int, device: torch.device) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Returns (H*W, d_model) positional encoding for an H×W patch grid."""
        rows = torch.arange(H, device=device)  # (H,)
        cols = torch.arange(W, device=device)  # (W,)
        row_embs = self.row_embed(rows)         # (H, d/2)
        col_embs = self.col_embed(cols)         # (W, d/2)
        # Broadcast: (H, 1, d/2) + (1, W, d/2) → (H, W, d/2) → concat → (H, W, d)
        row_b = row_embs.unsqueeze(1).expand(-1, W, -1)   # (H, W, d/2)
        col_b = col_embs.unsqueeze(0).expand(H, -1, -1)   # (H, W, d/2)
        pos = torch.cat([row_b, col_b], dim=-1)            # (H, W, d)
        return pos.view(H * W, -1)                         # (H*W, d)

pos_enc = Learned2DPosEncoding()
# For a 14×14 patch grid (196 patches):
pos = pos_enc(14, 14, device=torch.device("cpu"))
print(pos.shape)   # (196, 768)

: in Flamingo-style insertion, failing to initialise the gate α to zero causes immediate degradation of LLM capabilities. The gate ensures the model starts in a regime identical to the frozen baseline and learns visual conditioning gradually. Omitting it often prevents convergence entirely.

: in text-to-image generation the text embedding (K and V projections) does not change across denoising steps. Pre-computing and caching these projections reduces forward-pass cost by eliminating redundant linear projections at every step.

: when batching images of different sizes (or texts of different lengths), pad tokens must be masked out from the source sequence. A position that attends to padding with non-zero weight will contaminate the attended representation. Apply src_key_padding_mask in PyTorch's MultiheadAttention or manual -inf masking before the softmax.

: source and target modalities often have different embedding dimensions (e.g., CLIP ViT-L produces 1024-dim, but the LLM is 4096-dim). Always project both to a common d_model before computing attention. Using separate W_k and W_v projections from source_dim → d_model is standard.

: in long-context cross-attention (large S), attention weights can become nearly uniform if the temperature (√d_k) is wrong or the model has not converged. Monitor attention entropy during training; very high entropy indicates the cross-attention is not learning meaningful alignment. Flash Attention can help numerically at large S.
separates the source of queries from the source of keys and values, letting one modality extract information from another without requiring a shared token space or joint self-attention.

, gated cross-attention blocks (α=0 initialisation) let a frozen LLM learn visual conditioning without disrupting language capabilities. 64 Perceiver latents provide K, V at fixed cost regardless of image resolution.
, spatial feature map queries attend to 77 CLIP text tokens at every UNet block. Classifier-Free Guidance amplifies the conditional − unconditional score difference to steer generation faithfulness.
, 32 learnable query tokens use cross-attention to compress hundreds of ViT patch tokens into a fixed-size bottleneck, enabling any frozen LLM to ingest image information at constant cost.
for full joint self-attention — a significant saving when one sequence is much shorter than the other (Q-Former: 32 vs 196).
